title: What grit for oak before staining?
problem: Oak can show sanding scratches or uneven stain if the final grit is wrong.
likely_cause: Open-grain wood still needs a clean, even scratch pattern before stain. Stopping too coarse can show marks; sanding too fine can reduce stain penetration.
recommended_grit: Use 120 if leveling is needed, then 180 and 220 before staining.
steps: Sand with the grain using 120 only if the oak needs leveling.
Refine with 180.
Finish with 220 before stain.
Remove dust from the open grain before applying stain.
avoid: Do not jump from coarse sanding directly to stain.
success_check: The oak has an even scratch pattern and no visible cross-grain sanding marks.
quick_answer: For oak before staining, sand dry through 120, 180, and 220. Clean dust from the open grain before stain.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand across the grain before stain.
Do not leave sanding dust inside the open grain.
search_phrases: what grit for oak before staining

title: What grit for pine before staining?
problem: Pine can stain blotchy or show sanding marks after finishing.
likely_cause: Pine is soft, so coarse scratches and uneven pressure can show strongly after stain.
recommended_grit: Use 120 for rough areas, then 180 and 220 before staining.
steps: Sand with the grain using light pressure.
Use 120 only for rough or uneven areas.
Refine with 180, then 220.
Remove dust before stain.
avoid: Do not press hard on soft pine.
success_check: The pine feels even and has no visible sanding lines before stain.
quick_answer: For pine before staining, sand dry with 120 only where needed, then 180 and 220 with light pressure.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use heavy pressure on soft pine.
Do not stop at 120 before stain.
search_phrases: what grit for pine before staining

title: What grit removes pencil marks from wood?
problem: Pencil marks remain on wood before finishing.
likely_cause: Pencil graphite can sit in the grain and needs light sanding without creating a low spot.
recommended_grit: Use 180 or 220 for pencil marks. Use 120 only if the mark is deep or the area is rough.
steps: Try light dry sanding with 180 or 220.
Sand with the grain.
Blend the area into the surrounding surface.
Remove dust before finishing.
avoid: Do not dig a low spot into the wood just to remove a mark.
success_check: The pencil mark is gone and the area blends evenly with the surrounding wood.
quick_answer: Use 180 or 220 grit dry, sand with the grain, and blend the area so you do not create a low spot.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand one tiny spot aggressively.
Do not switch to stain until the mark and dust are gone.
search_phrases: what grit removes pencil marks from wood

title: How to sand rounded edges without flattening them
problem: Rounded edges become flat, sharp, or uneven after sanding.
likely_cause: A hard sanding block or heavy pressure can cut the high point of a rounded profile too quickly.
recommended_grit: Use 180 or 220 for shaping touch-up, then 320 for smoother prep.
wet_or_dry: Dry for wood and general prep.
steps: Use light pressure on the rounded edge.
Let the paper follow the curve instead of forcing it flat.
Use 180 or 220 to refine the edge.
Finish with 320 if the surrounding surface is also fine-prepped.
avoid: Do not press a hard block across a rounded edge.
success_check: The edge stays rounded and matches the surrounding scratch pattern.
quick_answer: Use light pressure and 180 or 220 grit. Let the paper follow the curve; do not force a hard block across the edge.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not focus pressure on the high point of the curve.
Do not over-sand edges during final prep.

title: What grit for trim molding before painting?
problem: Paint on trim can peel, show old brush marks, or fail to grip glossy molding.
likely_cause: Trim needs cleaning and scuff sanding so primer or paint can bond to the old finish.
recommended_grit: Use 180 or 220 for scuff sanding. Use 320 lightly between cured coats.
steps: Clean the trim before sanding.
Scuff glossy areas with 180 or 220.
Fold the paper for profiles and grooves.
Remove dust before primer or paint.
avoid: Do not paint glossy trim without scuff sanding.
success_check: The trim is clean, dull, and evenly scuffed before paint.
quick_answer: For trim before painting, clean first, then scuff with 180 or 220. Use folded paper for profiles.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand dirty trim.
Do not flatten decorative molding profiles.
search_phrases: what grit for trim before painting

title: What grit for baseboards before painting?
problem: Baseboard paint can peel or show scratches after repainting.
likely_cause: Baseboards collect dust, gloss, scuffs, and old paint ridges that need cleaning and light sanding.
recommended_grit: Use 180 or 220 for scuff sanding. Use 320 for light smoothing between cured coats.
steps: Clean dust and residue from the baseboard.
Scuff the surface with 180 or 220.
Use folded paper for grooves and tight edges.
Remove dust before painting.
avoid: Do not paint over dusty or glossy baseboards.
success_check: The baseboard is clean, dull, and smooth enough for paint.
quick_answer: For baseboards before painting, clean first, scuff with 180 or 220, then remove all dust.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand baseboards before cleaning them.
Do not leave dust in grooves before painting.
search_phrases: what grit for baseboards before painting

title: What grit for an interior door before painting?
problem: Painted doors can show old brush marks, glossy spots, or sanding scratches.
likely_cause: The door needs a clean, dull, even surface so primer or paint lays flat.
recommended_grit: Use 180 or 220 for scuff sanding, then 320 for finer smoothing if needed.
steps: Clean the door surface.
Sand old gloss with 180 or 220.
Blend brush marks or rough spots.
Remove dust before primer or paint.
avoid: Do not use coarse grit across a smooth visible door face unless stripping damaged finish.
success_check: The door face is dull, smooth, and free of dust before painting.
quick_answer: For an interior door before painting, clean it, scuff with 180 or 220, then use 320 for finer smoothing.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use 80 grit on a smooth door face for normal repainting.

search_phrases: what grit for interior door before painting

title: What grit for window trim before painting?
problem: Window trim paint can peel or show rough old edges after repainting.
likely_cause: Old paint ridges, gloss, and dust around trim need careful scuff sanding before paint.
recommended_grit: Use 180 or 220 for scuffing, then 320 for fine smoothing near visible edges.
steps: Clean the window trim.
Use 180 or 220 to dull glossy paint.
Use folded paper near profiles and corners.
Remove dust before painting.
avoid: Do not scratch glass or surrounding surfaces while sanding trim.
success_check: The trim is dull, clean, and smooth before paint.
quick_answer: For window trim before painting, use 180 or 220 to scuff, then 320 for fine visible edges.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand into glass with loose grit.

title: What grit removes old varnish?
problem: Old varnish remains on the surface after light sanding.
likely_cause: A fine grit may polish the varnish instead of cutting through the old finish.
recommended_grit: Use 80 or 120 for old varnish removal, then 180 and 220 for wood prep.
steps: Start with 80 or 120 only where varnish needs removal.
Sand evenly without digging into the wood.
Refine with 180.
Finish wood prep with 220 before applying a new finish.
avoid: Do not leave coarse removal scratches before refinishing.
success_check: Old varnish is removed and the wood is refined through 220.
quick_answer: Remove old varnish with 80 or 120, then refine the wood with 180 and 220 before new finish.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not stop after 80 or 120 grit.
Do not sand through veneer while removing old finish.

title: What grit between lacquer coats?
problem: Lacquer can show dust nibs, uneven texture, or adhesion issues between coats.
likely_cause: A cured coat may need very light leveling or scuffing before the next coat, depending on the finish instructions.
recommended_grit: Use 400 or 600 lightly between cured lacquer coats.
wet_or_dry: Dry for light scuffing; wet only when the lacquer system allows it.
steps: Let the lacquer coat cure as directed.
Sand lightly with 400 or 600.
Avoid cutting through edges.
Remove dust completely before recoating.
avoid: Do not sand uncured lacquer.
success_check: Dust nibs are flattened and the coat is clean before the next layer.
quick_answer: Between cured lacquer coats, sand very lightly with 400 or 600, then remove all dust before recoating.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand lacquer before it is ready.
Do not sand through corners or edges.

title: What grit between water-based finish coats?
problem: Water-based finish can feel rough after the first coat.
likely_cause: Water-based finishes can raise wood fibers or trap small dust nibs after the first coat.
recommended_grit: Use 320 or 400 lightly between cured water-based finish coats.
steps: Let the coat cure as directed.
Sand lightly with 320 or 400.
Focus on rough fibers or dust nibs.
Remove dust before the next coat.
avoid: Do not use heavy pressure between thin finish coats.
success_check: The finish feels smooth and is ready for the next coat.
quick_answer: Between cured water-based finish coats, use 320 or 400 lightly, then remove dust before recoating.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand through edges.
Do not recoat over sanding dust.

sandpaper for water based polyurethane between coats

title: What grit to scuff oil-based paint?
problem: New paint may not grip old oil-based paint well without surface prep.
likely_cause: Old oil-based paint can be glossy and slick, so it needs cleaning and scuff sanding before repainting.
recommended_grit: Use 180 or 220 for scuff sanding, then 320 if a smoother surface is needed.
steps: Clean the painted surface first.
Scuff glossy paint with 180 or 220.
Smooth visible areas with 320 if needed.
Remove dust before primer or paint.
avoid: Do not repaint glossy oil-based paint without proper prep.
success_check: The old paint is clean, dull, and evenly scuffed.
quick_answer: To scuff oil-based paint, clean first, then use 180 or 220. Use 320 for smoother visible areas.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand dirty or greasy paint.

title: What grit for metal railing before painting?

likely_cause: Metal railings need rust removal, loose paint removal, and a clean scuffed surface before primer or paint.
recommended_grit: Use 80 or 120 for rust and loose paint, then 180, 220, and 320 for paint prep.
steps: Remove loose rust or paint with 80 or 120.
Refine with 180 and 220.
Use 320 for smoother primer prep.
Clean dust and residue before coating.
avoid: Do not paint over active rust or loose old paint.
success_check: The railing is clean, stable, and evenly scuffed before primer.

Refine with 180 or 220.
Clean thoroughly before primer.

title: What grit for stainless steel sink scratches?

recommended_grit: Use 400 or 600 for scratch blending, then 800 or 1000 for finer matching.

Sand with the grain using 400 or 600.

Clean residue before inspecting.
avoid: Do not sand across the stainless grain.

title: What grit for aluminum wheel polishing prep?
problem: Aluminum wheels can stay scratched or dull after sanding.
likely_cause: Aluminum needs a step-by-step sanding sequence before polishing can create shine.
recommended_grit: Use 400 or 600 for prep, then 800, 1000, 1500, 2000, and 3000 before polishing.
wet_or_dry: Dry for early prep; wet for finer stages.
steps: Clean brake dust and residue first.
Start with 400 or 600 depending on scratch depth.
Refine through 800, 1000, 1500, 2000, and 3000.
Polish after the final sanding step.
avoid: Do not polish over deep 400- or 600-grit scratches.
success_check: The aluminum has a uniform fine haze before polishing.

title: What grit removes powder coat?
problem: Powder coat is hard and does not remove quickly with fine sandpaper.
likely_cause: Powder coating is a durable coating, so fine grits often only scuff the surface instead of removing it.
recommended_grit: Use 60 or 80 for removal, then 120, 180, and 220 for prep.
steps: Use 60 or 80 only for coating removal.
Sand evenly and avoid gouging the base material.
Refine with 120 and 180.
Finish prep with 220 before primer or repainting.
avoid: Do not expect 320 or finer to remove powder coat efficiently.
success_check: The powder coat is removed and the base surface is refined for the next coating step.
quick_answer: To remove powder coat, use 60 or 80 first, then refine with 120, 180, and 220 before repainting.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use ultra-fine grit for coating removal.

title: What grit removes dried glue from wood?

recommended_grit: Use 120 or 180 for dried glue, then 220 for final wood prep.

Blend into the surrounding wood.
Finish with 220 before stain or finish.
avoid: Do not leave glue residue before staining.
success_check: The glue spot is gone and the wood absorbs finish evenly.
quick_answer: Remove dried glue with 120 or 180, blend the spot, then finish with 220 before staining.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not stain over glue residue.
Do not sand one spot so deep that it becomes a low area.
search_phrases: what grit removes dried glue from wood
glue spot on wood after sanding

title: What grit for sanding drywall corners?

recommended_grit: Use 150 or 180 for joint compound corners, then 220 for light final smoothing.
steps: Let joint compound dry fully.
Use 150 or 180 with light pressure.
Feather the edges away from the corner.
Use 220 for final smoothing before primer.
avoid: Do not press hard into the corner line.
success_check: The corner is smooth, feathered, and ready for primer.

recommended_grit: Use 120 or 150 for leveling hard repair areas, then 180 or 220 before primer.
steps: Confirm the repair material is dry.
Use 120 or 150 only for high ridges.
Refine with 180 or 220.
Remove dust before primer.

title: What grit removes 3D print layer lines?
problem: 3D print layer lines remain visible after sanding.
likely_cause: Layer lines need a grit that levels the ridges before finer grits smooth the surface.
recommended_grit: Use 220 or 320 for layer lines, then 400, 600, and 800 for smoother prep.

steps: Start with 220 or 320 to reduce layer lines.
Use light pressure to avoid heat buildup.
Refine with 400, 600, and 800.
Use primer if a painted finish is planned.
avoid: Do not start with ultra-fine grit while layer ridges are still high.
success_check: Layer lines are reduced and the surface has an even scratch pattern.
quick_answer: For 3D print layer lines, start with 220 or 320, then refine with 400, 600, and 800.

problem: Primer on a 3D print shows rough spots, scratches, or layer lines.

title: Why does sanding make the finish look matte?
problem: A glossy finish turns matte or dull after sanding.
likely_cause: Sandpaper creates a uniform scratch pattern that scatters light. Fine sanding prepares for polishing or another coat, but it does not leave gloss by itself.
recommended_grit: Use 1500, 2000, and 3000 before polishing, or use 320 to 600 when preparing for another coat.
wet_or_dry: Wet for polishing prep; dry or wet for coating prep according to finish instructions.
steps: Identify whether the next step is polish or another coat.
For polish, refine through 1500, 2000, and 3000.
For another coat, use the grit recommended by the coating maker.
Clean the surface before polishing or recoating.
avoid: Do not expect sanding alone to restore gloss.
success_check: The finish has an even haze before polish or a clean scuff before recoating.
quick_answer: Sanding makes gloss look matte because it creates fine scratches. Polish after 3000 for shine, or recoat after proper prep.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not confuse sanding haze with final gloss.
Do not polish before deeper scratches are refined.
search_phrases: why does sanding make finish matte
how to restore shine after sanding
